citation_sentences: The estimated density is $7 . 0 7 8 ~ \mathrm { g / c m ^ { 3 } }$ and the measured density is $7 . 1 1 4 \ { \mathrm { g / c m } } ^ { 3 }$ . Fig. 1a and b presents the EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) map and phase map of the initial as-forged steel. The initial microstructure consists of equiaxed grains with fully austenitic constituent. The XRD patterns of as-forged steel and as-treated $\mathrm { a t } - 1 9 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ shown in Fig. 1c also suggest that the initial microstructure is fully austenitic and no isothermal martensite forms at $- 1 9 6 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ indicating a very stable austenite.

paper_title: Temperature dependence of the deformation behavior and mechanical properties of a Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel for cryogenic application
citation_sentences: The experimental steel was subjected to tensile deformation at $- 1 9 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , - 7 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and RT until fracture, respectively. The engineering stress-strain curves are plotted in Fig. 2a. Fig. 2b shows the corresponding true stress-strain curves and work hardening rate curves. It is observed that the work hardening rate decreases dramatically with strain at the initial stage of deformation, corresponding to the transition from elastic deformation to plastic deformation $[ 5 , 1 6 , 2 6 ]$ . Subsequently, the experimental steels deformed at − 77 and − ${ } ^ { 1 9 6 } { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ show a near-linear work hardening rate with strain, but a continuous decrease is observed at RT. The decreasing work hardening at RT has also been reported for other austenitic low-density steels [2]. In addition, as the deformation temperature decreases, the work hardening rate increases, indicating a negative temperature dependence of strength. The true strain at which true stress $\left( { \sigma } _ { \mathrm { t r u e } } \right)$ equals to work hardening rate $( \mathrm { { d } \sigma _ { t r u e } / \mathrm { { d } \varepsilon _ { t r u e } ) } }$ corresponds to the necking point. The tensile properties of the experimental steel at different temperatures are summarized in Fig. 2c. It is evident that the total elongation (TE) and uniform elongation (UE) increase with decreasing temperature. Additionally, decreasing deformation temperature leads to linear increase in both yield strength (YS) and ultimate tensile strength (UTS).

Normally, for high Mn steels, when the SFE is between 18 and 35 mJ/ $\mathtt { m } ^ { 2 }$ [42], mechanical twinning is the main deformation mechanism. Outside this range, DIMT and dislocation planar glide dominate deformation mechanism [42]. The SFE of the experimental steel at RT and − $- 7 7 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ is high enough that DIMT and mechanical twinning can be validly suppressed. When the deformation temperature decreases to − ${ } ^ { . 1 9 6 } { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ a SFE of 32 m $\mathrm { J } / \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }$ could still totally suppress DIMT but enable the occurrence of mechanical twinning. Therefore, as deformation temperature decreases from RT $\mathrm { t } 0 \ - 1 9 6 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the decreasing SFE promotes dislocation planar glide, resulting in the increasing strength and elongation (Fig. 2c).

caption: Fig. 3. EBSD analyses for the experimental steel deformed at different temperature: (a,d,g) − 196 ◦C; (b,e,h) − 77 ◦C; (c,f,i) RT. (a–c) IPF maps; (d–f) GB maps; (g–i) GND distribution maps superposed with GB maps for the region marked with frame in (d–f). In IPF maps, the colors indicate crystal directions parallel to the tensile direction. Mechanical twins are marked with arrows. In grain boundary distribution maps, the fractions of LAGBs and HAGBs are marked with red and black colors.

paper_title: Temperature dependence of the deformation behavior and mechanical properties of a Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel for cryogenic application
citation_sentences: The tensile deformed microstructure of the uniform deformation section was analyzed by EBSD. Fig. 3 presents the IPF maps and grain boundary (GB) distribution maps for the tensile deformed microstructure. The IPF maps as presented in Fig. 3a-c shows that the crystal orientation of deformed austenite grains concentrates in {111} and {001}, which is a result of coordinated plastic deformation. However, as deformation temperature decreases $\mathrm { t } 0 \ - 1 9 6 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the decreasing SFE leads to small amount of mechanical twins formed during tensile deformation, as marked with arrows in Fig. 3a. The occurrence of mechanical twins is confirmed by TEM analysis as shown in Fig. 4a. It is seen that the mechanical twinning plane formed along <112> crystal direction on {111} plane in the case of the grain oriented near <110> zone axis. Compared with the sample deformed at − ${ } ^ { . 1 9 6 } \ { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ no mechanical twins but microbands are observed in the sample deformed at − ${ \bf - 7 7 ^ { \circ } C } ,$ , as shown in Fig. 4b. A typical characteristic of planar glide that the uniform and dense distribution of microbands present in the deformed microstructure, which has been widely reported in austenitic low density steels [27,28]. The corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern indicates that these bands are orientated along {111} planes. The fractions of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) and low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs) are marked in the grain boundary distribution maps in Fig. 3d-f. It can be seen that the LAGBs mainly concentrate at these austenite grain with finer grain size. Additionally, the fraction of LAGBs dramatically increases with the decreasing temperature. The geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) distribution maps superposed with GB maps for the region marked with frame in Fig. 3d-f are shown separately in $\mathrm { F i g . ~ } 3 { \bf g - i . }$ It is seen that the GNDs accumulate at LAGBs, indicating a more severe local deformation of these regions.

citation_sentences: The tensile deformed microstructure of the uniform deformation section was analyzed by EBSD. Fig. 3 presents the IPF maps and grain boundary (GB) distribution maps for the tensile deformed microstructure. The IPF maps as presented in Fig. 3a-c shows that the crystal orientation of deformed austenite grains concentrates in {111} and {001}, which is a result of coordinated plastic deformation. However, as deformation temperature decreases $\mathrm { t } 0 \ - 1 9 6 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ the decreasing SFE leads to small amount of mechanical twins formed during tensile deformation, as marked with arrows in Fig. 3a. The occurrence of mechanical twins is confirmed by TEM analysis as shown in Fig. 4a. It is seen that the mechanical twinning plane formed along <112> crystal direction on {111} plane in the case of the grain oriented near <110> zone axis. Compared with the sample deformed at − ${ } ^ { . 1 9 6 } \ { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ no mechanical twins but microbands are observed in the sample deformed at − ${ \bf - 7 7 ^ { \circ } C } ,$ , as shown in Fig. 4b. A typical characteristic of planar glide that the uniform and dense distribution of microbands present in the deformed microstructure, which has been widely reported in austenitic low density steels [27,28]. The corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern indicates that these bands are orientated along {111} planes. The fractions of high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) and low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs) are marked in the grain boundary distribution maps in Fig. 3d-f. It can be seen that the LAGBs mainly concentrate at these austenite grain with finer grain size. Additionally, the fraction of LAGBs dramatically increases with the decreasing temperature. The geometrically necessary dislocation (GND) distribution maps superposed with GB maps for the region marked with frame in Fig. 3d-f are shown separately in $\mathrm { F i g . ~ } 3 { \bf g - i . }$ It is seen that the GNDs accumulate at LAGBs, indicating a more severe local deformation of these regions.

It is widely accepted that the mechanical properties of materials depend on the plastic deformation mechanism of austenite, which is strongly SFE-dependent $\left[ 5 , 1 6 \right] . \ \mathrm { F i g } . \ 7$ illustrates the generalized temperature (or SFE) dependence of the tensile elongation of austenitic steels [32]. At high SFE (region I), the three-dimensional mobility of perfect dislocations is enhanced. The dominant deformation mechanism is wavy glide, which leads to the formation of dislocation cells [33]. The dislocation cells cause strain localization and stress concentration, thus resulting in a low elongation. When SFE is lower (region II), perfect dislocations separate into two extended Shockley dislocations with wide separation [34]. In such a case, dislocations have a low three-dimensional mobility, which means that the dislocations slipping is restricted on a single slip plane [17,35]. In region $\mathrm { I I } ,$ the SFE is so low that wavy glide is absent but not low enough to initiate DIMT. In the relatively higher SFE range in region II, mechanical twins are usually formed as shown in Fig. 4a. At relatively lower SFE range, the dislocation planar slip results in the formation of glide bands as shown in Fig. 4b. The product of planar glide such as mechanical twins and glide bands could act as barriers for the movement of partial dislocations (Fig. 4), thus contributing to a high work hardening rate. Dislocation planar glide is easier than wavy glide at low temperature. The lower the deformation temperature, the higher the glide planarity [16]. High glide planarity is conducive to the dislocation multiplication and movement, and therefore continuous uniform deformation [36]. Therefore, the tensile elongation increases with the decreasing SFE. Planar glide except DIMT dominates the deformation mechanism of these steels with moderate SFE such as TWIP steels [37–39] and austenitic low-density steels $[ 2 7 , 4 0 , 4 1 ]$ . When the SFE is in region III, DIMT is the dominant deformation mechanism. The occurrence of DIMT leads to a high strength but also a brittleness. Lower SFE promotes DIMT, therefore the elongation decreases with the decreasing temperature.

citation_sentences: According to the ISO evaluation procedure [46], the experimental curves obtained $\mathrm { a t } - 1 9 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , - 7 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and RT are processed and separately presented in Fig. 9a-c. The characteristic values associated with impact properties can be obtained based on the ISO evaluation curves. The general yield load $\mathrm { ( F _ { g y } ) }$ is at the transition point from elastic deformation to plastic deformation. The maximum Charpy impact load $\left( \mathrm { F } _ { \mathrm { m } } \right)$ corresponds to the peak load of the load-deflection curves.
Crack initiation leads to a slope change of CCR curve shown in Fig. 9, where the corresponding load is referred as crack initiation load $( \mathrm { F _ { i n } } ) .$ .

For the load-deflection curve obtained $\mathbf { a t } - 1 9 6 ^ { \circ } \mathbf { C } , \mathrm { F } _ { \mathrm { i n } }$ is almost equal to $\mathrm { F } _ { \mathrm { m } }$ because no obvious change happens to the slope of compliance changing rate curve. The start of sharp near-linear decrease in the loaddeflection curve is related to the beginning of unstable crack growth and the corresponding load is referred as $\mathrm { F _ { i u } }$ . The load corresponding to the end of unstable crack growth is referred as $\mathrm { F _ { a } } .$ The remaining section after $\mathrm { F _ { a } }$ corresponds to the final collapse stage, unrelated to the impact cracking mechanism [47]. The changes of $\mathrm { F _ { g y } , F _ { m } , F _ { i n } }$ and $\mathrm { F _ { i u } / F _ { m } }$ with testing temperature are plotted in Fig. 9d. It can be found from Fig. 9d that the change of $\mathrm { F _ { g y } }$ shows a negative dependence of temperature, which is in good agreement with the increase in YS with the decreasing temperature. In addition, as temperature decreases, $\mathrm { F _ { m } }$ increases, indicating that the experimental steel could withstand larger impact load at lower temperatures. A larger $\mathrm { F _ { i n } }$ at lower temperature indicates that it is more difficult for crack initiation. The value of $\mathrm { F _ { i u } / F _ { m } }$ could reflect the development of cracks. A larger $\mathrm { F _ { i u } / F _ { m } }$ at lower temperature suggests that cracks rapidly propagate from stable to unstable regime. Rebound compliance (RC) is the absolute value of the curve slope from $\mathrm { F _ { i u } }$ to $\mathrm { F _ { a } }$ (as shown in Fig. 9a-c), which reflects the unstable crack growth rate. It can be seen that RC is larger at lower temperature, which is a hint that unstable cracks propagate more rapidly at lower temperature.

The absorbed impact energy associated with different stages of crack initiation and growth, i.e., crack initiation $( \mathsf { W } _ { \mathrm { i n i t i a t i o n } } ) ,$ , stable crack growth $\left( \mathsf { W } _ { \mathrm { s t a b l e } } \right) _ { : }$ , unstable crack growth $( \mathrm { W _ { u n s t a b l e } } )$ , and final collapse $( \mathsf { W _ { c o l l a p s e } } ) _ { : }$ , are analyzed following the methods in Refs [46], as shown in Fig. 9. The $W _ { \mathrm { i n i t i a t i o n } }$ at $- 1 9 6 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } , - 7 7 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and RT is 112, 68 and $^ { 5 7 , J , }$ respectively. The increasing $\mathsf { W _ { i n i t i a t i o n } }$ with decreasing temperature reveals that the resistance of the experimental steel to crack initiation is stronger at lower temperature. A high level of $W _ { \mathrm { i n i t i a t i o n } }$ is the key factor for maintaining good cryogenic impact toughness [11]. Additionally, as temperature decrease, obvious decreases in both $\mathsf { W } _ { \mathsf { s t a b l e } }$ and Wunstable can be observed, implying that the resistance to crack propagation is weaker at lower temperature. According to the above, the experimental steel exhibits a higher crack initiation energy upon cryogenic impact, which contributes to good cryogenic toughness. However, the lower crack propagation energy reduces the overall cryogenic toughness of the experimental steel.

paper_title: Temperature dependence of the deformation behavior and mechanical properties of a Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel for cryogenic application
citation_sentences: The microstructure of the cross-section near fracture surface was analyzed by EBSD, as presented in Fig. 10. For samples tested at − 196 ◦C and RT, the primary crack propagation paths at the fracture surface exhibit a zigzag shape. Most regions exhibit the characteristic of transgranular fracture. Misorientation analysis along the marked line for grain A is presented in Fig. 10c. The misorientation difference between the regions on both sides of the crack is less than 15◦, implying that the two regions are in the same grain before the crack occurred. This suggests that transgranular fracture occurred in this region. In this case, crack propagation leads to relatively severe plastic deformation near the primary cracks. As a result, a significant local orientation gradient occurs in the region adjacent to the primary cracks within the grain interiors. Besides, secondary cracks present in the sample tested at RT, which is different from that at − 196 ◦C. The presence of secondary cracks release stress concentration at the tip of the primary cracks, making stress redistribute within material. Hence, impact fracture is delayed, contributing to a better toughness [47,48]. This explains that the crack propagation energy is higher at RT.

paper_title: Temperature dependence of the deformation behavior and mechanical properties of a Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel for cryogenic application
citation_sentences: It is worth noting that the experimental steel exhibits a higher tensile elongation but a lower toughness as deformation temperature decreases. The SFE reduces with the decreasing temperature, increasing glide planarity during low strain rate deformation. A high glide planarity benefits continuous uniform deformation, therefore increasing the tensile elongation [36]. On the other hand, the experimental steel exhibited a poor cryogenic impact toughness, which is opposite to the finding by Liu et al. [49] that FCC-structured high-entropy alloys show higher toughness at cryogenic temperature. The mechanical twinning occurs easily in high-entropy alloys because of lower SFE. The poor cryogenic impact toughness of the experimental steel is related to the higher activation energy for slip systems at lower temperature [29]. Although a lower temperature is corresponding to a higher glide planarity at a low strain rate, activating planar glide is difficult at low temperature during high strain rate deformation because of the higher active energy [29]. As shown in Fig. 11, the characteristic of planar glide can be observed in the regions near fracture. Noted that only few regions experienced planar glide during impact deformation at − ${ 1 9 6 } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ as shown in Fig. 11a. As temperature increases, planar glide becomes more pronounced. These planar glide features could retard the propagation of crack, thus increasing toughness [50,51]. Therefore, the resistance to crack propagation is weaker at lower temperature.
